sections.title: Overview
sections.content: "Return of spontaneous circulation" (ROSC) refers to the resumption of a sustained, effective heart rhythm and blood circulation after a cardiac arrest. It's a crucial goal of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and is indicated by signs like breathing, coughing, movement, a palpable pulse, or measurable blood pressure. Post-resuscitation care refers to the medical management provided after a patient achieves return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) following cardiac arrest. The goal is to stabilise the patient, prevent further complications, and optimise recovery.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: - Return of spontaneous circulation post cardiac arrest

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Unless there are compelling reasons to move immediately, do not rush
- Continue to work as a team and anticipate re-arrest
- The focus of post-resuscitation care is on oxygenation, ventilation, perfusion, temperature control, and identifying the cause of arrest
- ROSC patients are often unstable initially. Plan packaging, handling, and egress carefully and avoid rough movement where possible
- Use this time to ensure monitoring is secure, lines are secure, and 12 lead ECGs are obtained

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Re-evaluate Primary Survey
- Reassess oxygenation and ventilation continuously
- Provide ventilatory support if required
- Titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 around 94 to 98% and avoid routine hyperoxia where possible
- Avoid hyperventilation and excessive inflation, as these may impair venous return and cerebral perfusion
- Perform frequent vital signs, including ETCO2 where available
- In patients who remain comatose after ROSC, monitor temperature and actively prevent fever where feasible
- If not already contacted, ensure consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider and address precipitating causes of arrest
- Perform serial 12 lead ECGs because the first post-ROSC tracing may be non-diagnostic
- Manage hypotension promptly:
  - Consider IV or IO crystalloid while reassessing for fluid responsiveness and signs of overload
  - Aim initially for adequate perfusion, commonly SBP above 100 mmHg or MAP at least 60 to 65 mmHg, adjusted to the clinical context where known
  - If hypotension persists, seek early medical advice regarding vasopressor support and continue frequent reassessment
- **Temperature management:** Avoid hyperthermia post-ROSC - fever significantly worsens neurological outcomes. Target normothermia (36–37.5°C). Active cooling may be required if temperature exceeds 37.5°C. Targeted temperature management (TTM) protocols are initiated at the receiving hospital, but pre-hospital temperature monitoring and avoidance of overheating should begin immediately after ROSC

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All ROSC patients require a 12 lead ECG
- Adrenaline 0.1 mg/mL solution: 1 mg diluted to 10 mL with sodium chloride 0.9%
- Adrenaline 0.05 mg/mL solution: 1 mg diluted to 20 mL with sodium chloride 0.9%
- Prepare for handover: Downtime/time of arrest, shocks delivered, dosages of medication administered, etc
